title: Squadron Leader X

infobox.value: [Miles Malleson](/wiki/Miles_Malleson "Miles Malleson") [Wolfgang Wilhelm](/wiki/Wolfgang_Wilhelm_(writer) "Wolfgang Wilhelm (writer)") [Emeric Pressburger](/wiki/Emeric_Pressburger "Emeric Pressburger")
story "Four Days in a Hero's Life" by Emeric Pressburger
Victor Hanbury Productions (for) [RKO Radio British Productions](/wiki/RKO_Pictures "RKO Pictures")

*Squadron Leader X* is named the third "most wanted" of all, behind only *[The Mountain Eagle](/wiki/The_Mountain_Eagle "The Mountain Eagle")* ([Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock")) and *[Two Crowded Hours](/wiki/Two_Crowded_Hours "Two Crowded Hours")* ([Michael Powell](/wiki/Michael_Powell "Michael Powell")).[[6]](#cite_note-BFI-6)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1943 British film, now lost

| Squadron Leader X | |
| --- | --- |
| [U.S. promotional poster](/wiki/Film_poster "Film poster") | |
| Directed by | [Lance Comfort](/wiki/Lance_Comfort "Lance Comfort") |
| Written by | [Miles Malleson](/wiki/Miles_Malleson "Miles Malleson") [Wolfgang Wilhelm](/wiki/Wolfgang_Wilhelm_(writer) "Wolfgang Wilhelm (writer)") [Emeric Pressburger](/wiki/Emeric_Pressburger "Emeric Pressburger") |
| Based on | story "Four Days in a Hero's Life" by Emeric Pressburger |
| Produced by | [Victor Hanbury](/wiki/Victor_Hanbury "Victor Hanbury") |
| Starring | [Eric Portman](/wiki/Eric_Portman "Eric Portman") [Ann Dvorak](/wiki/Ann_Dvorak "Ann Dvorak") |
| Cinematography | [Mutz Greenbaum](/wiki/Mutz_Greenbaum "Mutz Greenbaum") |
| Edited by | [Michael C. Chorlton](/wiki/Michael_C._Chorlton "Michael C. Chorlton") |
| Music by | [William Alwyn](/wiki/William_Alwyn "William Alwyn") (composer) [Muir Mathieson](/wiki/Muir_Mathieson "Muir Mathieson") (conductor) |
| Production company | Victor Hanbury Productions (for) [RKO Radio British Productions](/wiki/RKO_Pictures "RKO Pictures") |
| Distributed by | [RKO Radio British Productions](/wiki/RKO_Pictures "RKO Pictures") |
| Release date | * 1 March 1943 (1943-03-01) (U.K.) |
| Running time | 99 minutes |
| Country | United Kingdom |
| Language | English |

***Squadron Leader X*** is a 1943 British World War II spy drama directed by [Lance Comfort](/wiki/Lance_Comfort "Lance Comfort") and starring [Eric Portman](/wiki/Eric_Portman "Eric Portman") and [Ann Dvorak](/wiki/Ann_Dvorak "Ann Dvorak").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The screenplay was adapted by [Miles Malleson](/wiki/Miles_Malleson "Miles Malleson") and Wolfgang Wilhelm from a short story by [Emeric Pressburger](/wiki/Emeric_Pressburger "Emeric Pressburger").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The [BFI National Archive](/wiki/BFI_National_Archive "BFI National Archive") considers the film "missing, believed lost".

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Squadron_Leader_X&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

Erich Kohler ([Eric Portman](/wiki/Eric_Portman "Eric Portman")), a crack [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") pilot who speaks fluent English, is ordered by his superior, Inspector Siegel ([Frederick Richter](/wiki/Friedrich_Richter_(actor) "Friedrich Richter (actor)")), to drop a "stick" of bombs on the Belgian city of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"). He is further instructed to bail out of his aircraft wearing a British [RAF](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") uniform, gain the confidence of the local populace and then try to convince them that the British are responsible for the bombing of civilian targets in Belgium.

Despite being able to have a convincing English accent, and equipped with a photograph of his "wife" and a packet of [Players](/wiki/John_Player_%26_Sons "John Player & Sons") cigarettes, the plan goes awry when Kohler falls into the hands of the Belgian Resistance. The resistance members believe they are doing him a favour by arranging for him to be smuggled to Britain among a group of downed RAF bomber crew who are being returned that night.

On arriving in Britain, Kohler escapes and makes his way to London where he tries to get in touch with old contacts, only to find that most have been interned on the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man"). He does manage, however, to contact British nurse Barbara Lucas ([Ann Dvorak](/wiki/Ann_Dvorak "Ann Dvorak")), an old flame who once had Nazi sympathies, but is not willing to help him. Kohler then takes refuge with the Krohns ([Martin Miller](/wiki/Martin_Miller_(actor) "Martin Miller (actor)") and [Beatrice Varley](/wiki/Beatrice_Varley "Beatrice Varley")), a couple who are reluctant Nazi agents due to threats being made of harm to family members in Germany if they fail to co-operate.

Kohler finds himself being hunted both by the British [MI5](/wiki/MI5 "MI5") and by German officials furious at his bungled mission in Belgium. Dr. Schultz ([Henry Oscar](/wiki/Henry_Oscar "Henry Oscar")), a ruthless [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo") officer, confronts and accuses Kohler of inefficiency and cowardice. A shoot-out follows and Schultz is killed.

Meanwhile, MI5 agent Inspector Milne ([Walter Fitzgerald](/wiki/Walter_Fitzgerald "Walter Fitzgerald")) picks up Kohler's trail. Kohler manages narrowly to avoid arrest and steals a [Supermarine Spitfire](/wiki/Supermarine_Spitfire "Supermarine Spitfire") fighter aircraft in which to fly back to Germany. Over the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"), he is spotted by German fighters who believe they are engaging a British pilot, and shoot the aircraft down.

## Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Squadron_Leader_X&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast")]

* [Eric Portman](/wiki/Eric_Portman "Eric Portman") as Erich Kohler
* [Ann Dvorak](/wiki/Ann_Dvorak "Ann Dvorak") as Barbara Lucas
* [Walter Fitzgerald](/wiki/Walter_Fitzgerald "Walter Fitzgerald") as Inspector Milne
* [Martin Miller](/wiki/Martin_Miller_(actor) "Martin Miller (actor)") as Mr. Krohn
* [Beatrice Varley](/wiki/Beatrice_Varley "Beatrice Varley") as Mrs. Krohn
* [Henry Oscar](/wiki/Henry_Oscar "Henry Oscar") as Dr. Schultz
* [Barry Jones](/wiki/Barry_Jones_(actor) "Barry Jones (actor)") as Bruce Fenwick
* [Charles Victor](/wiki/Charles_Victor "Charles Victor") as Marks
* [Marjorie Rhodes](/wiki/Marjorie_Rhodes "Marjorie Rhodes") as Mrs. Agnew
* [David Peel](/wiki/David_Peel_(actor) "David Peel (actor)") as Michael Bertholt
* [Aubrey Mallalieu](/wiki/Aubrey_Mallalieu "Aubrey Mallalieu") as Pierre
* [Mary Merrall](/wiki/Mary_Merrall "Mary Merrall") as Miss Thorndike
* [Carl Jaffe](/wiki/Carl_Jaffe "Carl Jaffe") as Luftwaffe Colonel
* [Frederick Richter](/wiki/Friedrich_Richter_(actor) "Friedrich Richter (actor)") as Inspector Siegel

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Squadron_Leader_X&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

*Squadron Leader X* featured a Supermarine Spitfire fighter aircraft and a [Klemm Kl 25](/wiki/Klemm_Kl_25 "Klemm Kl 25") light aircraft.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In a contemporary review, *The Cinema* declared: "The air-sequences, made with official [Air Ministry](/wiki/Air_Ministry "Air Ministry") co-operation, are among the most actionful and breath-taking ever screened".

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Squadron_Leader_X&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Reception")]

*Squadron Leader X* was exceptionally well received by some critics on its release, with Lionel Collier in his review for the *[Picturegoer](/wiki/Picturegoer "Picturegoer")*, named it, "one of the best spy melodramas yet made". The *[Monthly Film Bulletin](/wiki/Monthly_Film_Bulletin "Monthly Film Bulletin")* spoke of "a cast that is of the highest order" and summarised the film as "an exciting story, well scripted and produced with tremendous attention to detail".

Film historian Bertil Skogsberg in *Wings on the Screen: A Pictorial History of Air Movies* (1987) called some sequences "fairly exciting but otherwise (an) uninteresting thriller."[[4]](#cite_note-4) Aviation film historian James H. Farmer in *Celluloid Wings: The Impact of Movies on Aviation* (1984) had a similar appraisal, that, *Squadron Leader X* was "overlong".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Preservation status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Squadron_Leader_X&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Preservation status")]

There is no record of *Squadron Leader X* ever being re-shown after its original release. The [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") has been unable to trace a print for inclusion in the [BFI National Archive](/wiki/BFI_National_Archive "BFI National Archive"), and currently classes the film as "missing, believed lost". *Squadron Leader X* is included on the BFI's "[75 Most Wanted](/wiki/BFI_75_Most_Wanted "BFI 75 Most Wanted")" list of missing British feature films.[[6]](#cite_note-BFI-6) Film historians in the 21st century have also evinced a major increase in interest in Comfort's directorial career and *Squadron Leader X* is a vital missing piece of his filmography.[[N 1]](#cite_note-7)
